options.text: Implement clustering in BigQuery on the package-tracking ID column.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement clustering in BigQuery on the ingest date column.

options.label: D
options.text: Tier older data onto Cloud Storage files and create a BigQuery table using Cloud Storage as an external data source.

cueWhy: Queries by package lifecycle not ingest day → cluster on package-tracking ID.

answerPattern: A: Cluster the table by country and username fields.
questionText: Your company's customer_order table in BigQuery stores the order history for 10 million customers, with a table size of 10 PB. You need to create a dashboard for the support team to view the order history. The dashboard has two filters, country_name and username. Both are string data types in the BigQuery table. When a filter is applied, the dashboard fetches the order history from the table and displays the query results. However, the dashboard is slow to show the results when applying the filters to the following query:How should you redesign the BigQuery table to support faster access?

options.label: A
options.text: Cluster the table by country and username fields.

options.label: B
options.text: Cluster the table by country field, and partition by username field.

options.label: C
options.text: Partition the table by country and username fields.

options.label: D
options.text: Partition the table by _PARTITIONTIME.

cueWhy: Filter-heavy huge order table → cluster by country and username.

answerPattern: A: Create a Cloud Storage bucket with Autoclass enabled.
questionText: You are planning to use Cloud Storage as part of your data lake solution. The Cloud Storage bucket will contain objects ingested from external systems. Each object will be ingested once, and the access patterns of individual objects will be random. You want to minimize the cost of storing and retrieving these objects. You want to ensure that any cost optimization efforts are transparent to the users and applications. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create a Cloud Storage bucket with Autoclass enabled.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition objects from Standard to Coldline storage class if an object age reaches 30 days.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition objects from Standard to Coldline storage class if an object is not live.

options.label: D
options.text: Create two Cloud Storage buckets. Use the Standard storage class for the first bucket, and use the Coldline storage class for the second bucket. Migrate objects from the first bucket to the second bucket after 30 days.

cueWhy: One-write random-read cost optimization without app changes → Autoclass.

answerPattern: C: Use Dataflow, write a pipeline that reads the data from Kafka, and writes the data to BigQuery.
questionText: Your infrastructure team has set up an interconnect link between Google Cloud and the on-premises network. You are designing a high-throughput streaming pipeline to ingest data in streaming from an Apache Kafka cluster hosted on- premises. You want to store the data in BigQuery, with as minimal latency as possible. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Setup a Kafka Connect bridge between Kafka and Pub/Sub. Use a Google-provided Dataflow template to read the data from Pub/Sub, and write the data to BigQuery.

options.label: B
options.text: Use a proxy host in the VPC in Google Cloud connecting to Kafka. Write a Dataflow pipeline, read data from the proxy host, and write the data to BigQuery.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Dataflow, write a pipeline that reads the data from Kafka, and writes the data to BigQuery.

options.label: D
options.text: Setup a Kafka Connect bridge between Kafka and Pub/Sub. Write a Dataflow pipeline, read the data from Pub/Sub, and write the data to BigQuery.

cueWhy: On-prem Kafka over interconnect into BQ with low latency → Dataflow.

answerPattern: A: Use Analytics Hub to facilitate data sharing.
questionText: You are migrating your on-premises data warehouse to BigQuery. As part of the migration, you want to facilitate cross-team collaboration to get the most value out of the organization’s data. You need to design an architecture that would allow teams within the organization to securely publish, discover, and subscribe to read-only data in a self-service manner. You need to minimize costs while also maximizing data freshness. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Analytics Hub to facilitate data sharing.

options.label: B
options.text: Create authorized datasets to publish shared data in the subscribing team's project.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a new dataset for sharing in each individual team’s project. Grant the subscribing team the bigquery.dataViewer role on the dataset.

options.label: D
options.text: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to copy datasets to a centralized BigQuery project for sharing.

cueWhy: Org-wide self-serve read-only sharing with freshness → Analytics Hub.

answerPattern: C: Invoke a Cloud Function instance that uses Python to apply the logic on your JSON file.
questionText: You are using Workflows to call an API that returns a 1KB JSON response, apply some complex business logic on this response, wait for the logic to complete, and then perform a load from a Cloud Storage file to BigQuery. The Workflows standard library does not have sufficient capabilities to perform your complex logic, and you want to use Python's standard library instead. You want to optimize your workflow for simplicity and speed of execution. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create a Cloud Composer environment and run the logic in Cloud Composer.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a Dataproc cluster, and use PySpark to apply the logic on your JSON file.

options.label: C
options.text: Invoke a Cloud Function instance that uses Python to apply the logic on your JSON file.

options.label: D
options.text: Invoke a subworkflow in Workflows to apply the logic on your JSON file.

cueWhy: When Workflows can’t run complex Python logic, call a Cloud Function then continue.

answerPattern: A: Schedule a daily export of the table to a Cloud Storage dual or multi-region bucket.
questionText: You are using BigQuery with a regional dataset that includes a table with the daily sales volumes. This table is updated multiple times per day. You need to protect your sales table in case of regional failures with a recovery point objective (RPO) of less than 24 hours, while keeping costs to a minimum. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Schedule a daily export of the table to a Cloud Storage dual or multi-region bucket.

options.label: B
options.text: Schedule a daily copy of the dataset to a backup region.

options.label: C
options.text: Schedule a daily BigQuery snapshot of the table.

options.label: D
options.text: Modify ETL job to load the data into both the current and another backup region.

cueWhy: Cheap regional-table protection: daily export to dual/multi-region GCS.

answerPattern: B: Create a materialized view in BigQuery that pre-calculates the aggregations and filters used in the Looker Studio dashboards. | D: Use BigQuery BI Engine to accelerate query performance by caching frequently accessed data.
questionText: Your company uses Looker Studio connected to BigQuery for reporting. Users are experiencing slow dashboard load times due to complex queries on a large table. The queries involve aggregations and filtering on several columns. You need to optimize query performance to decrease the dashboard load times. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure Looker Studio to use a shorter data refresh interval to ensure fresh data is always displayed.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a materialized view in BigQuery that pre-calculates the aggregations and filters used in the Looker Studio dashboards.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement row-level security in BigQuery to restrict data access and reduce the amount of data processed by the queries.

options.label: D
options.text: Use BigQuery BI Engine to accelerate query performance by caching frequently accessed data.

cueWhy: Precompute with materialized views and/or accelerate with BI Engine for Looker Studio.

answerPattern: B: 1. Use Dataplex to organize the BigQuery datasets and Cloud Storage buckets into lakes and zones. 2. Use Dataplex for automated metadata discovery, centralized security policy management, data profiling, and data quality tasks.
questionText: Your company has data assets across multiple Cloud Storage buckets and BigQuery datasets containing raw and processed data. The requirement is to establish a unified data governance framework that allows for centralized metadata discovery, data quality monitoring, and consistent security policy application across these various data stores without physically moving or duplicating the data. You need to implement a solution to achieve this federated governance. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: 1. Export metadata out of Dataplex Universal Catalog by running a metadata export job. 2. Implement Dataproc Metastore to manage table schemas and Apache Hive metastore for metadata discovery. 3. Manage security using a combination of BigQuery row-level security and Cloud Storage policies.

options.label: B
options.text: 1. Use Dataplex to organize the BigQuery datasets and Cloud Storage buckets into lakes and zones. 2. Use Dataplex for automated metadata discovery, centralized security policy management, data profiling, and data quality tasks.

options.label: C
options.text: 1. Deploy a centralized Cloud SQL database to store metadata extracted from BigQuery and Cloud Storage using custom scripts. 2. Integrate the database with Looker Studio for data discovery and visualization. 3. Implement a custom policy engine using Cloud Run functions triggered by changes in IAM policies to enforce consistent security across projects.

options.label: D
options.text: 1. Create a Looker Studio dashboard on BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA views to visualize and monitor data quality. 2. Manage security using IAM policies at the project level, supplemented by BigQuery authorized views for granular access control.

cueWhy: Federated governance over GCS+BQ without copies is Dataplex lakes/zones + quality/policies.

answerPattern: B: Export the data to CSV files, upload the files to Cloud Storage, then load the files into BigQuery.
questionText: Your company is planning to migrate a large on-premises data warehouse to BigQuery. The data is currently stored in a proprietary, vendor-specific format. You need to perform a batch migration of this data to BigQuery. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the bq command-line tool to load the data directly from the on-premises data warehouse.

options.label: B
options.text: Export the data to CSV files, upload the files to Cloud Storage, then load the files into BigQuery.

options.label: C
options.text: Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Datastream to replicate the data in real time.

cueWhy: Unknown proprietary warehouse format: export CSV → GCS → load BigQuery.

answerPattern: D: Create Google Groups for access levels, assign developers to groups, and grant the groups the appropriate IAM roles on each project.
questionText: Your company uses separate Google Cloud projects for development, staging, and production. Developers need edit access in the development project, read-only access in the staging project, and no access in the production project. You need an effective and manageable solution to assign and enforce these permissions according to Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create separate VPC Service Controls perimeters for each project and use access levels.

options.label: B
options.text: Grant individual developers specific IAM roles directly on each project.

options.label: C
options.text: Configure firewall rules within each project’s VPC to block traffic from unauthorized developer machines.

options.label: D
options.text: Create Google Groups for access levels, assign developers to groups, and grant the groups the appropriate IAM roles on each project.

cueWhy: Manage env IAM via Google Groups with roles per project, not per-user bindings.

answerPattern: B: Implement a Cloud Composer DAG, with each step defined as a separate task using appropriate Airflow operators, and schedule the DAG for daily execution.
questionText: Your financial services company has a critical daily reconciliation process that involves several distinct steps: fetching data from an external SFTP server, decrypting the files, loading them into Cloud Storage, and finally running a series of BigQuery SQL transformations. Each step has strict dependencies, and the entire process should notify you if not completed by 7:00 AM. Manual intervention for failures is costly and delays compliance reporting. You need a highly observable and robust solution that supports easy re-runs of individual steps if errors occur. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Develop a Cloud Composer DAG that includes a single PythonOperator to execute a Python script that runs each step sequentially, incorporating error handling and retries. Upload the scripts to a Cloud Composer environments DAGs folder, and configure it to run daily.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement a Cloud Composer DAG, with each step defined as a separate task using appropriate Airflow operators, and schedule the DAG for daily execution.

options.label: C
options.text: Define a Cloud Composer DAG to orchestrate the SFTP fetch and decryption steps, and then use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a separate Dataflow job that handles the Cloud Storage load and BigQuery transformations and schedule to run daily.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a Cloud Composer DAG that includes a single BashOperator to execute a top-level shell script, which in turn calls individual scripts for each pipeline step. Upload the scripts to a Cloud Composer environment’s DAGs folder, and configure it to run daily.

cueWhy: Multi-step SFTP→decrypt→GCS→BQ with SLA/alerts and reruns is Cloud Composer (Airflow).

answerPattern: A: Use BigQuery editions to create an autoscaling reservation with dedicated slots for analyst dashboards, while running ETL jobs with a separate reservation or using the on-demand billing model.
questionText: Your large e-commerce company uses BigQuery for its data warehousing needs. They have two primary types of workloads:•	Analyst dashboards: Interactive queries run by business analysts throughout the day, requiring low latency for real-time insights.•	ETL jobs: Large, complex batch queries that load and transform data overnight. These jobs are cost-sensitive and can tolerate longer execution times, and they do not have the same performance requirements as the dashboards.Analysts are reporting inconsistent performance of their dashboards, particularly during peak business hours. You need to ensure reliable performance for both workload types while optimizing costs. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use BigQuery editions to create an autoscaling reservation with dedicated slots for analyst dashboards, while running ETL jobs with a separate reservation or using the on-demand billing model.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement a custom autoscaling solution using Cloud Run functions to dynamically adjust available BigQuery slots based on time of day.

options.label: C
options.text: Migrate all ETL jobs to Cloud SQL to reduce BigQuery’s workload.

options.label: D
options.text: Run all analyst dashboard queries as interactive queries and configure ETL jobs to run as batch queries.

cueWhy: Separate capacity: reserved/autoscaling slots for interactive dashboards vs separate/on-demand for ETL.

answerPattern: A: Move from BigQuery on-demand to slot reservations.
questionText: Your team runs a complex analytical query daily that processes terabytes of data. Recently, after running for 20 minutes, the query fails with a “Resources exceeded” error. You need to resolve this issue. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Move from BigQuery on-demand to slot reservations.

options.label: B
options.text: Analyze the SQL syntax for errors.

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the maximum table size limit.

options.label: D
options.text: Increase your project’s BigQuery API request quota.

cueWhy: On-demand resource failures on huge queries → move to slot reservations.

answerPattern: C: Convert the unstructured documents into high-dimensional numerical vectors that capture the semantic meaning and relationships of the text.
questionText: Your company wants to implement a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to allow employees to query an extensive knowledge base of internal documents, such as policy manuals and project reports. You need to prepare this unstructured text for embedding to be used in the RAG system. What should you do to ensure the system can retrieve the most relevant information?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Cloud Data Loss Prevention (Cloud DLP) to scan and redact sensitive information within the documents before processing.

options.label: B
options.text: Store the documents as compressed files in a traditional relational database to enable more efficient storage and retrieval.

options.label: C
options.text: Convert the unstructured documents into high-dimensional numerical vectors that capture the semantic meaning and relationships of the text.

options.label: D
options.text: Index each word from the documents into a search engine to enable keyword-based search.

cueWhy: RAG prep means embedding unstructured text into semantic vectors, not keyword indexes alone.

answerPattern: A: Create an external table in BigQuery pointing to the Cloud Storage bucket and run the INSERT INTO ... SELECT * FROM external_table command.
questionText: You need to load a dataset with multiple terabytes of clickstream data into BigQuery. The data arrives each day as compressed JSON files in a Cloud Storage bucket. You need a low-cost, programmatic, and scalable solution to load the data into BigQuery. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create an external table in BigQuery pointing to the Cloud Storage bucket and run the INSERT INTO ... SELECT * FROM external_table command.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service from Cloud Storage.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a Cloud Run function to run a Python script to read and parse each JSON file, and use the BigQuery streaming insert API.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Cloud Data Fusion to create a pipeline to load the JSON files into BigQuery.

cueWhy: TB-scale daily JSON in GCS: external table then INSERT…SELECT is cheap and scalable.

answerPattern: B: Configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule to transition objects older than two years to the Archive storage class and eventually delete them after seven years.
questionText: Your Cloud Storage data lake has raw, processed, and historical data in different buckets. Data older than two years is rarely accessed, and all data must be retained for no longer than seven years. You are concerned about rising storage costs. How should you control costs for the historical data bucket?

options.label: A
options.text: Write a script on a Compute Engine instance, triggered daily by Cloud Scheduler, to scan all objects and delete any older than seven years.

options.label: B
options.text: Configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule to transition objects older than two years to the Archive storage class and eventually delete them after seven years.

options.label: C
options.text: Enable the Autoclass feature on your Cloud Storage buckets and select Opt-in to object transitions to Coldline and Archive storage classes.

options.label: D
options.text: Replicate the buckets to a different region with lower storage costs and configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule to delete objects after seven years.

cueWhy: Lifecycle: Archive after 2 years, delete at 7 years for rarely accessed historical objects.

answerPattern: D: Deploy a Dataproc cluster with Hive services enabled.
questionText: Your data science team needs to perform interactive SQL queries on large datasets stored in Apache Parquet format within a Cloud Storage bucket. The team is familiar with Apache Hive and wants to leverage existing HiveQL queries. You need to provide an environment for the team to run their interactive HiveQL queries directly against the data in Cloud Storage. You want to keep operational overhead to a minimum. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Install and configure an Apache Hadoop and Hive cluster manually on a group of Compute Engine instances.

options.label: B
options.text: Load the Parquet data into a BigQuery native table and use the BigQuery Connector for Hive to run the queries.

options.label: C
options.text: Configure BigQuery with an external table definition pointing to the Parquet files.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy a Dataproc cluster with Hive services enabled.

cueWhy: Interactive HiveQL on Parquet in GCS with low ops → Dataproc with Hive enabled.

answerPattern: C: Use Pub/Sub for ingestion, Dataflow with Apache Beam for processing, and BigQuery for storage and analytics.
questionText: You analyze user clickstream data to personalize content recommendations. The data arrives continuously and needs to be processed with low latency, including transformations such as sessionization (grouping clicks by user within a time window) and aggregation of user activity. You need to identify a scalable solution to handle millions of events each second and be resilient to late-arriving data. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Firebase Realtime Database for ingestion and storage, and Cloud Run functions for processing and analytics.

options.label: B
options.text: Use Cloud Data Fusion for ingestion and transformation, and Cloud SQL for storage and analytics.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Pub/Sub for ingestion, Dataflow with Apache Beam for processing, and BigQuery for storage and analytics.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Cloud Storage for ingestion, Dataproc with Apache Spark for batch processing, and BigQuery for storage and analytics.

cueWhy: High-rate clickstream with sessionization and late data is Pub/Sub + Dataflow + BigQuery.

answerPattern: D: Partition the table by the event date column and cluster the table by user ID column.
questionText: You are designing BigQuery tables for large volumes of clickstream event data. Your data analyst team will most frequently query by specific event date ranges and filter by the user ID UUID. You want to optimize table structure for query cost and performance. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Partition the table by the user ID column and cluster the table by the event date column.

options.label: B
options.text: Create an ingestion-time partitioned table and cluster it by the user ID column.

options.label: C
options.text: Cluster the table by both the event date and the user ID columns.

options.label: D
options.text: Partition the table by the event date column and cluster the table by user ID column.

cueWhy: Partition by event date and cluster by user ID optimizes time-range + user filters.

answerPattern: D: Apply a ParDo transform in Dataflow to validate each element, use a RunInference transform to assign sentiment scores, and use side outputs to route valid/invalid records.
questionText: You are building a Dataflow pipeline to ingest customer feedback. Before loading to your data warehouse, you must validate email addresses and enrich unstructured comment strings with a generative AI sentiment classification. Invalid records need to be routed for manual review. How should you implement this pipeline?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure the data source system to pre-validate data before sending it to Dataflow and use the BigQuery ML.GENERATE_TEXT command to assign a sentiment score.

options.label: B
options.text: Use Dataflow to load all data into BigQuery and execute a SQL MERGE statement to flag invalid records and BigQuery ML to assign sentiment scores.

options.label: C
options.text: After the Dataflow load completes, execute a Cloud Run function to scan for invalid entries and call Vertex AI to assign sentiment scores.

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a ParDo transform in Dataflow to validate each element, use a RunInference transform to assign sentiment scores, and use side outputs to route valid/invalid records.

cueWhy: Dataflow ParDo validation + RunInference sentiment + side outputs for invalids matches the branching pipeline.

answerPattern: D: Select Cloud Workflows.
questionText: You need to orchestrate a pipeline with several Google Cloud services: a batch Dataflow job, then a BigQuery query job followed by a Vertex AI batch prediction. The logic is sequential. You want a lightweight, serverless orchestration solution with minimal operational overhead. What service should you use?

options.label: A
options.text: Select Cloud Composer.

options.label: B
options.text: Select Compute Engine with cron.

options.label: C
options.text: Select Dataproc with Apache Oozie.

options.label: D
options.text: Select Cloud Workflows.

cueWhy: Sequential Dataflow→BQ→Vertex with minimal ops is Cloud Workflows, not heavy Composer.

answerPattern: D: Create a pipeline by using BigQuery pipelines and schedule it to load the data into BigQuery daily.
questionText: You have a data analyst team member who needs to analyze data by using BigQuery. The data analyst wants to create a data pipeline that would load 200 CSV files with an average size of 15MB from a Cloud Storage bucket into BigQuery daily. The data needs to be ingested and transformed before being accessed in BigQuery for analysis. You need to recommend a fully managed, no-code solution for the data analyst. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create a Cloud Run function and schedule it to run daily using Cloud Scheduler to load the data into BigQuery.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to load files from Cloud Storage to BigQuery, create a BigQuery job which transforms the data using BigQuery SQL and schedule it to run daily.

options.label: C
options.text: Build a custom Apache Beam pipeline and run it on Dataflow to load the file from Cloud Storage to BigQuery and schedule it to run daily using Cloud Composer.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a pipeline by using BigQuery pipelines and schedule it to load the data into BigQuery daily.

cueWhy: Analyst no-code daily CSV→BQ load/transform fits BigQuery pipelines.

answerPattern: A: Launch the Dataflow job with the --region=us-central1 parameter.
questionText: You are designing a stateful data processing pipeline that reads data from a Cloud Storage bucket and writes transformed data to a BigQuery table. The pipeline must be highly available and resilient to zonal failures within the us-central1 region. You need to configure a Dataflow pipeline ensuring minimal disruption during a zonal outage. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Launch the Dataflow job with the --region=us-central1 parameter.

options.label: B
options.text: Deploy the Dataflow job to a single zone within us-central1 and configure it to use a regional persistent disk to store its state.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy the Dataflow job to a single zone within us-central1 and use a multi-regional Cloud Storage bucket to store its state.

options.label: D
options.text: Launch the Dataflow job with the --zone=us-central1a parameter.

cueWhy: Regional Dataflow (--region=us-central1) survives zonal outages better than a single zone.

answerPattern: A: Publish the datasets as listings within BigQuery sharing (Analytics Hub).
questionText: Your team has created several BigQuery curated datasets containing anonymized industry benchmark data. You want to make these datasets easily discoverable and accessible for querying by external partner companies within their own Google Cloud projects. You need a secure and scalable solution. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Publish the datasets as listings within BigQuery sharing (Analytics Hub).

options.label: B
options.text: Export the datasets to partner-specific Cloud Storage buckets.

options.label: C
options.text: Create authorized views for each dataset and grant access to each partner.

options.label: D
options.text: Grant the roles/bigquery.dataViewer IAM role to the partner group email addresses on the datasets.

cueWhy: Secure cross-org discoverable sharing of curated BQ datasets is Analytics Hub listings.

answerPattern: D: Use AEAD functions and delete keys when employees leave the organization.
questionText: Your organization stores highly personal data in BigQuery and needs to comply with strict data privacy regulations. You need to ensure that sensitive data values are rendered unreadable whenever an employee leaves the organization. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use column-level access controls with policy tags and revoke viewer permissions when employees leave the organization.

options.label: B
options.text: Use dynamic data masking and revoke viewer permissions when employees leave the organization.

options.label: C
options.text: Use customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) and delete keys when employees leave the organization.

options.label: D
options.text: Use AEAD functions and delete keys when employees leave the organization.

cueWhy: Crypto-shredding: AEAD encrypt with per-employee keys and delete keys when they leave.

answerPattern: D: Leverage BigQuery's Query History view and analyze the execution graph.
questionText: You monitor and optimize the BigQuery instance for your team. You notice that a particular daily report that uses a large JOIN operation is consistently slow. You want to examine the query's execution plan to identify potential performance bottlenecks within the JOIN as quickly as possible. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the bg query --dry_run command to review the estimated number of bytes read and review query syntax.

options.label: B
options.text: Run a query on the INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT view filtering by the job_ID and analyze total_bytes_processed.

options.label: C
options.text: Review the BigQuery audit logs in Cloud Logging.

options.label: D
options.text: Leverage BigQuery's Query History view and analyze the execution graph.

cueWhy: To inspect JOIN bottlenecks fast, use Query History and the execution graph.

answerPattern: C: Configure BigQuery cross-region dataset replication from ns-central1 to us-east1.
questionText: Your company stores vital operational sales data in a BigQuery dataset in us-central1. Your company requires a disaster recovery plan to restore this data to us-east1 with a recovery point objective (RPO) of 24 hours and a recovery time objective (RTO) of 4 hours if us-central1 experiences an outage. You need to implement the disaster recovery plan while keeping costs and complexity to a minimum. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Set up continuous queries and Pub/Sub to stream data changes from BigQuery tables in us-central1 to us-east1.

options.label: B
options.text: Manually export data to a CSV file in a multi-regional Cloud Storage bucket daily and use bq load to restore to us-east1.

options.label: C
options.text: Configure BigQuery cross-region dataset replication from ns-central1 to us-east1.

options.label: D
options.text: Take daily BigQuery table snapshots in us-central1.

cueWhy: Cross-region BigQuery dataset replication to us-east1 meets regional DR with low ops vs custom export.

answerPattern: B: Configure a scheduled transfer in BigQuery Data Transfer Service to ingest data from the MySQL database with a scheduled transfer configuration.
questionText: You are integrating your legacy on-premises MySQL database into a new Google Cloud data warehouse. The database contains historical customer loyalty data which needs to be extracted daily and loaded into BigQuery. You want a managed integration solution that is secure and supports incremental loads. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a data pipeline using Pub/Sub for streaming the data changes from MySQL.

options.label: B
options.text: Configure a scheduled transfer in BigQuery Data Transfer Service to ingest data from the MySQL database with a scheduled transfer configuration.

options.label: C
options.text: Leverage Database Migration Service for continuous replication of the entire database to Cloud SQL.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Cloud SQL federated queries to directly query the on-premises MySQL database from BigQuery.

cueWhy: Daily MySQL→BigQuery managed incremental ingest is BigQuery Data Transfer Service.

answerPattern: C: Use the TRANSFORM clause within the CREATE MODEL statement, leveraging SQL functions for aggregations and time-based calculations.
questionText: You are developing a fraud detection model using BigQuery ML. You have a raw transaction dataset and need to create new features such as the average_transaction_amount_last_24_hours and time_since_last_transaction. These features require aggregation and time-window calculations on the existing data. The goal is to ensure that these features are consistently applied during both model training and prediction without manual intervention. You need to prepare these features efficiently for your model. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a Cloud Run function that triggers on new transactions, calculates the features, and inserts them into a feature store before model serving.

options.label: B
options.text: Export the BigQuery data to Cloud Storage, perform feature engineering using a custom Python script in a Dataflow job, and then re-import the engineered features into BigQuery.

options.label: C
options.text: Use the TRANSFORM clause within the CREATE MODEL statement, leveraging SQL functions for aggregations and time-based calculations.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a separate BigQuery table containing pre-computed features using complex SQL queries and join this table with the raw data during model training and serving.

cueWhy: BQML TRANSFORM in CREATE MODEL keeps feature SQL consistent for train and predict without manual dual pipelines.

answerPattern: C: Use Vertex AI Vision API to analyze the images and store the results in BigQuery.
questionText: Your organization has thousands of images stored in Cloud Storage. The organization's data analysts need to categorize these images upon analyzing the image content and store the results in a structured format for integration with other BigQuery datasets. You need a solution that allows the analysts to perform this task with the least amount of coding. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the Gemini 2.5 Pro model in Vertex AI to analyze the images and store the results in BigQuery.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the ML.UNDERSTAND_TEXT function along with object tables to analyze the images, and store the JSON output in a Cloud Storage bucket.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Vertex AI Vision API to analyze the images and store the results in BigQuery.

options.label: D
options.text: Use the ML.GENERATE_TEXT function along with object tables to analyze the images and store the JSON output in a BigQuery table.

cueWhy: Least-code image content analysis into structured BigQuery results points to Vertex AI Vision API.

answerPattern: D: Select Spanner.
questionText: You are building a system to process financial transactions. The system must handle a high throughput of concurrent user operations and each operation requires low-latency reads and writes to individual records. You need to identify a storage solution that guarantees ACID compliance for the processed transactions. You want a Google Cloud managed service. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Select Bigtable.

options.label: B
options.text: Select BigQuery.

options.label: C
options.text: Select Cloud Storage.

options.label: D
options.text: Select Spanner.

cueWhy: Concurrent transactional ops with ACID on a managed service maps to Spanner.

answerPattern: B: Replicate the exact same pre-processing logic in the inference pipeline that was used during model training.
questionText: You are preparing data to serve a sales demand prediction model. The training data undergoes several pre-processing steps, including scaling numerical features and one-hot encoding categorical features. The model is deployed on Vertex AI Endpoints. You need to prevent training-serving skew and ensure accurate predictions in production. You want a solution that is easy to implement.What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a custom handler within the Vertex AI Endpoint to automatically perform data transformations before the model makes a prediction.

options.label: B
options.text: Replicate the exact same pre-processing logic in the inference pipeline that was used during model training.

options.label: C
options.text: Store the raw, unprocessed data in a separate Cloud Storage bucket exclusively for serving.

options.label: D
options.text: Ensure the serving data is a smaller, random sample of the training data.

cueWhy: Preprocessing must match train and serve. Replicate the same pre-processing logic in the Vertex AI inference path.

answerPattern: B: Create a materialized view to minimize repetitive computations.
questionText: Your retail company is concerned about their BigQuery analytics spend. The company runs several queries that require the use of the same aggregation for the store ID and real-time sales volume. You need to implement the optimal solution that minimizes analytics spend and returns faster results. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Create a new table from a CSV file with the repeated aggregation for the other queries to reference for faster processing.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a materialized view to minimize repetitive computations.

options.label: C
options.text: Use join acceleration with primary and foreign keys to increase query joining to live data.

options.label: D
options.text: Leverage partitioning to minimize the number of bytes read.

cueWhy: Repeated store-ID/sales aggregations should not recompute every query. A materialized view caches that aggregation cheaper and faster.

answerPattern: D: Create separate datasets with authorized views exposing only approved data.
questionText: You are defining the data governance strategy for a new BigQuery table with medical and financial data. You want a scalable solution that ensures the clinical researchers can access patient medical data without financial information, while allowing the accounting team to access only financial data with minimal patient identifiers. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement column-level security policies in BigQuery tables with IAM permissions.

options.label: B
options.text: Create separate tables for personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, and anonymized medical data. Use IAM permissions to control access to each table.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement row-level security policies in BigQuery tables with IAM permissions.

options.label: D
options.text: Create separate datasets with authorized views exposing only approved data.

cueWhy: Different teams need different columns from the same sensitive table. Separate datasets with authorized views expose only approved fields per team.

answerPattern: C: Use Cloud Storage and BigQuery as repositories. Use Dataplex Universal Catalog for metadata discovery, data quality checks, and transformations.
questionText: You are designing a data lake on Google Cloud to store vast amounts of customer interaction data from various sources, such as websites, mobile apps, and social media. You need to ensure that this data, which arrives in different formats, is consistently cataloged and easy for data analysts to discover and use. You also want to perform basic data quality checks and transformations before the data is consumed by downstream applications. You need an automated and managed data governance solution. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Cloud Storage as the central repository. Use Vertex AI to classify and process the data and perform data quality checks.

options.label: B
options.text: Stream all the data directly into BigQuery, where it is automatically cataloged and governed.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Cloud Storage and BigQuery as repositories. Use Dataplex Universal Catalog for metadata discovery, data quality checks, and transformations.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Cloud Storage as the central repository. Use a Cloud Run function to catalog, transform the data, and perform data quality checks.

cueWhy: Different formats need catalog discovery plus quality/transform before use. Dataplex Universal Catalog over GCS/BQ is the managed governance path.

answerPattern: D: Use session windows with a 30-minute gap duration.
questionText: You are using Dataflow to build a streaming data pipeline to analyze user website click activity from Pub/Sub. You need to calculate the number of clicks for each user site visit. A site visit is defined as a period of activity followed by 30 minutes of inactivity for a specific user. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use tumbling windows with a 30-minute window.

options.label: B
options.text: Use hopping windows with a 30-minute window, and a 1-minute period.

options.label: C
options.text: Use hopping windows with a 30-minute window, and a 30-minute period.

options.label: D
options.text: Use session windows with a 30-minute gap duration.

cueWhy: A visit ends after inactivity, which is a per-user gap — not a fixed clock window. Session windows with a 30-minute gap match that definition; tumbling/hopping windows do not.
